title: US Strikes Iranian Bridges as War Escalates, Ukraine Ousts Defence Minister in Political Crisis, China Purges Top Civilian Leader
subhead: The US hits Iranian civilian infrastructure for the first time in the current campaign, Vance accuses Israel of manipulating American opinion to prolong the war, and Zelenskyy sacks his popular defence minister — triggering street protests and a leadership crisis amid Russia's invasion.

sections.heading: US Strikes Iranian Bridges and Boarded Tanker as Sixth Night of Attacks Begins
sections.body:
The US launched its sixth consecutive night of strikes on Iran, with US Central Command saying the operations were intended to 'further degrade Iranian military capabilities'. But this round marked a significant escalation: Iranian state media reported US missiles struck two bridges in Hormozgan province — the first confirmed attacks on civilian infrastructure in the current fighting. The BBC verified an attack on one bridge west of Bandar Abbas. Strikes also hit near Qeshm Island, Bandar Abbas, and Bushehr, where Iran's nuclear power plant is located.

President Trump had threatened earlier in the week to hit Iranian bridges and power plants if Tehran did not return to negotiations — a threat that UN human rights chief Volker Türk previously warned could constitute war crimes under the 1949 Geneva Conventions. Iran's foreign ministry accused the US of a 'barbaric attack' after a children's cancer hospital in Ahvaz had to be evacuated due to strikes nearby.

The US also boarded an oil tanker in the Gulf of Oman as part of its renewed blockade of Iranian ports, which began on Tuesday. Centcom said it had 'redirected three commercial vessels trying to run the blockade'. According to Centcom, the previous US blockade between April and June disabled nine ships and redirected over 140. Iran's top negotiator, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, said Tehran had 'no reason' to abide by any agreement that did not benefit Iran, calling control of the Strait of Hormuz an 'inviolable red line'.
sections.whyItMatters: Striking bridges — civilian infrastructure — represents a deliberate widening of the US campaign beyond military targets. It signals Trump is following through on threats that international lawyers warned could violate international humanitarian law, raising the stakes for both the conflict and its legitimacy.

sections.heading: Vance Accuses Israel of 'Manipulating' US Public Opinion to Prolong Iran War
sections.body:
In an interview with Joe Rogan published on Wednesday, Vice-President JD Vance accused members of the Israeli government of 'manipulating and trying to change American public opinion to keep the war going on indefinitely'. Vance said there was 'exact evidence' that some Israeli leaders 'hate the deal' — the US-Iran memorandum of understanding signed last month — and were running a 'very discreet, extremely well-funded campaign to try to derail the negotiation'.

White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said Trump 'would certainly agree that foreign countries certainly do try to persuade American public opinion'. The Israeli government did not immediately respond. Vance's comments are the latest in a string of criticisms of Israel from the vice-president, who in June urged Israeli leaders not to attack 'the only powerful ally you have anywhere left in the entire world'.

Meanwhile, the US House of Representatives voted 314-104 against cutting billions in aid to Israel, though several Democrats broke ranks — reflecting a shift within the party away from unconditional support. Israel continues to demand the complete removal of Iran's nuclear and missile programmes as well as regime change, positions that are incompatible with the US-Iran MOU framework.
sections.whyItMatters: A US vice-president publicly accusing an ally of covertly manipulating American opinion to prolong a war is remarkable. It signals a genuine rift within the US-Israel relationship over the war's endgame — one that could reshape diplomatic dynamics if the administration pursues a deal over Israel's objections.

sections.heading: Ukraine Sacks Defence Minister as Protests Erupt Over Military Leadership
sections.body:
President Volodymyr Zelenskyy dismissed Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov on Wednesday, triggering the largest anti-government protests since Russia's 2022 invasion. Over 1,000 people gathered in Kyiv and other cities, chanting 'Shame!' and 'Syrskyi out!', referring to Commander-in-Chief Oleksandr Syrskyi, whom Zelenskyy chose to retain over Fedorov.

Fedorov, 35, confirmed that his removal stemmed from his proposal to replace Syrskyi and Chief of General Staff Andrii Hnatov. He accused Syrskyi of running the military on 'loyalty' rather than data, blocking reform initiatives, and using 'Soviet-style' methods. 'Instead of finding a way of defeating Russia asymmetrically — which is the commander-in-chief's job — he's found a way of splitting our country,' Fedorov said. A prominent drone unit commander resigned in protest, calling the sacking 'a great evil for the country's defence capability'.

Zelenskyy appointed Yevhenii Khmara, acting head of the Security Service (SBU), as acting defence minister, saying Khmara had 'unprecedented experience with technological combat operations'. The crisis overshadowed UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer's farewell visit to Kyiv, where he told Zelenskyy that Putin was 'losing' and Ukraine would win.
sections.whyItMatters: Sacking a popular, reformist defence minister mid-war — and backing the old-guard commander over him — risks demoralising Ukraine's forces and alienating Western partners who saw Fedorov as a moderniser. It also exposes a deep institutional rift between reformers and the military establishment that could hamper Ukraine's battlefield effectiveness.

sections.heading: Xi Purges Top Civilian Politburo Member as Anti-Corruption Campaign Deepens
sections.body:
China expelled Ma Xingrui, one of the Communist Party's most senior officials, making him the third Politburo member purged since 2022 and the first civilian to fall in the latest wave. Ma, formerly the party secretary of Xinjiang, was accused of corruption, abuse of power, and trading political favours for sex. He has not been seen since the announcement.

Experts noted that the charges against Ma lacked the 'politically charged' language — such as 'two-faced person' or 'disloyalty' — seen in previous purges, including those of military officials Zhang Youxia and He Weidong. Christopher Nye of the Jamestown Foundation said this suggests 'Xi Jinping's political tolerance has diminished', and that corruption allegations alone are now sufficient grounds for removal, even without evidence of political opposition.

The reference to Ma's misconduct dating from the period after the CCP's 18th National Congress in 2012 — when Xi took power — is notable. 'Before that, Xi wasn't interested. But anything that happened after that can now become grounds for a purge,' Nye said. Several of Ma's former subordinates have also come under investigation.
sections.whyItMatters: The purge of a civilian Politburo member — without the usual political framing — signals that Xi's tolerance for dissent has narrowed further. No tier of leadership is safe, and the anti-corruption campaign is now a tool for enforcing loyalty across both military and civilian leadership.

sections.heading: Canadian Wildfires Blanket US Cities as Smoke Reaches World Cup Final Venue
sections.body:
More than 850 wildfires are burning across Canada, the vast majority out of control, sending hazardous smoke across the northern United States. Detroit recorded the worst air quality of any city in the world on Thursday, followed by Minneapolis, Chicago, and Toronto, according to IQAir. Air quality in parts of Michigan and Minnesota was classified as 'hazardous', with residents urged to stay indoors. New York activated air quality emergency protocols and distributed KN95 masks citywide.

Smoke is now drifting toward New Jersey, where the World Cup final is scheduled for Sunday, raising concerns about player and spectator health. A First Nations community in northern Ontario has been 'burnt to ashes', according to its chief, Helen Paavola, who told CityNews an aerial flyover showed 'all the homes are gone'. Republican lawmakers in Michigan sent an open letter to Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney expressing frustration for a third consecutive year, saying 'nothing has changed except that our patience has run out'. Carney responded that both countries have a responsibility to fight climate change.

Ontario Premier Doug Ford dismissed criticism, saying 150 fire crews were on the ground and 'we will spend whatever it takes'. Researchers note that fires have become more frequent since 2015, with the burning pattern migrating eastward from western Canada into Ontario, Quebec, and Atlantic provinces.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the third consecutive year of severe Canadian wildfire smoke affecting millions of Americans. It is no longer an anomaly — it is a recurring climate-driven disruption to public health, daily life, and even international sporting events, with political pressure mounting on both sides of the border.

sections.heading: Rubio Calls for Global Crackdown on Far-Left Extremism
sections.body:
Secretary of State Marco Rubio hosted representatives from over 60 countries in Washington to urge collaboration against what he called the growing threat of left-wing terrorism, calling it a 'transnational' issue requiring shared intelligence. The Trump administration has already designated Antifa as a domestic terrorist group and added four European organisations — including Antifa Ost (Germany) and Greece's Revolutionary Class Self-Defense — to its Foreign Terrorist Organization list.

White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller characterised leftists as 'fundamentally motivated by envy, by hatred, by jealousy'. The state department announced visa restrictions targeting members of 'Far-Left Terrorist' groups, including those allegedly involved in 'economic sabotage'.

Eleven Democratic lawmakers wrote to Rubio questioning whether the focus on left-wing groups might target lawful protests and political opponents, calling it 'politically partisan'. A 2025 CSIS report found that left-wing terrorism had surpassed far-right terrorism for the first time in 30 years in the US, though incidents from both sides remained relatively low. However, the Justice Department quietly removed a 2024 internal study showing far-right extremists were responsible for the bulk of ideologically motivated deaths.
sections.whyItMatters: The US is now formally prioritising far-left extremism as a global security threat and building an international coalition around it — while a sitting administration is simultaneously removing data that contradicts its framing. The move has domestic political implications ahead of midterms and raises questions about the politicisation of counter-terrorism policy.

bottomLine: The US-Iran conflict entered a new phase as American strikes hit Iranian civilian bridges for the first time — crossing a line Trump had publicly threatened and international lawyers had warned could violate the laws of war. Vice-President Vance openly accused Israel of manipulating American opinion to prolong the conflict, exposing a widening rift between Washington and Tel Aviv over the war's endgame. In Ukraine, Zelenskyy's decision to sack his popular defence minister triggered the largest domestic protests since the invasion, revealing a deep institutional fracture between reformers and the military old guard. Xi Jinping purged a top civilian Politburo member — the first civilian to fall — signalling that no rank is immune and that loyalty enforcement is tightening. And Canadian wildfires made global headlines for the third straight summer, with smoke now threatening the World Cup final and displacing entire communities.
